meta.title: Apollo 16 Astronaut Charlie Duke's Incredible Keynote at the Astro Awards
meta.description:
Brought to you on behalf of the United States Space Force! Thanks so much for helping us get this legend to the 2nd Annual 2025 Astro Awards! @U.S.SpaceForce 

Tickets available for the 3rd annual Astro Awards show on January 17th and 18th in Austin, TX!!! - http://astroawards.live

Enjoy Apollo 16 Lunar Module Pilot, Moon Walker, Test Pilot and absolute legend Charlie Duke's inspiring keynote speech from the 2nd Annual Astro Awards on January 26th, 2025.

--------------------------

Want to support what I do? Consider becoming a Patreon supporter for access to exclusive livestreams, our discord channel! - http://patreon.com/everydayastronaut

Or become a YouTube member for some bonus perks as well! - https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC6uKrU_WqJ1R2HMTY3LIx5Q/join

All music is original! Find it anywhere you listen to music! (Spotify, iTunes, Google Play, Amazon, etc)

transcript.text: [music]
Brigadier General Charles Duke, Apollo
[music] 16 astronaut, US Air Force
officer, and one of only 12 humans to
walk on the moon, is with us today.
His story is one of service, courage,
and exploration at the highest level.
Charlie's journey to space began as a
fighter pilot, a graduate of the US
Naval Academy, and later an Air Force
test pilot. His precision and skill
paved the way for his selection as an
astronaut for NASA's Apollo program.
In 1972, as a lunar module pilot for
Apollo 16, Charlie became the youngest
person to step foot on the moon at just
36 years old. Now, I want to pause and
say, I'm 40. What have I been doing with
my life?
While on the lunar surface, he conducted
experiments, collected samples, and
contributed data that continues to shape
our understanding of the moon to this
day.
But to me, one of the most remarkable
things about Charlie is the way he
connects with people. I'll never forget
meeting him almost exactly eight years
ago. Tomorrow, it will be exactly 8
years ago to today with my then
six-year-old daughter, Millie. Like many
kids, she was fascinated by space and
thrilled to meet an astronaut.
She asked him what he did on the moon,
and when he replied, "I collected
rocks." Her face fell into a pout and
she declared, "That's not fair. you got
to go to the moon and I didn't.
I feel the exact same way.
That moment captured the essence of who
Charlie is. Yes, he's a legend of space
exploration, but he's also someone whose
warmth and humility have inspired
generations to dream bigger and aim
higher.
Even after the Apollo 16 mission,
Charlie continued to serve and advance
NASA's efforts, leaving a legacy of
leadership that paved the way for the
future of human space flight. His
contributions remind us of what can be
achieved through dedication, courage,
and a belief in the impossible.
As we look ahead to new frontiers,
whether it's returning to the moon,
reaching for Mars, or safeguarding our
assets in orbit, we are fortunate to
stand on the shoulders of pioneers like
Charlie.
So, without further ado, please join me
in welcoming a living legend, a man
whose achievements have left an
indelible mark on the history of
exploration, Apollo 16 astronaut,
General Charles Duke. [applause]
Thank you very much. [applause]
Thank you a lot. Thank you for great
introduction. Thank you so much. Thank
you.
[cheering] Thank you very much. Thank
you.
Thanks so much. Please be seated.
It's a great uh pleasure being here. I
want to thank the space US Space Force
for uh sponsoring uh uh this event and
our trip. And so, uh, getting to meet,
uh, those who are now recruiting for
the, uh, you, uh, Air Force for the
Space Force. It's, uh, great meeting
you. Uh, they worked at the recruiting
service at Randolph and where I worked
my last, uh, eight years in the Air
Force Reserve. So, it's, uh, good to I
was recruiting 18 year olds back in
those days. Uh it was uh in the early uh
uh let's see or uh '7s I guess before
that.
My career is just going uh in my mind
now trying to get the years straight.
But uh anyway uh it's good to be here. I
I want to talk about Apollo
uh and how it got started uh and a
little bit about uh my career. Uh I tell
all a lot of young people I said just
keep your antennas up because you never
know where your career is going to take
you. Uh and uh sensitive to urges uh and
uh at the Naval Academy I had this urge
to I just loved airplanes and they gave
me some rides in airplanes. So uh uh I
decided to back then you could go in the
air force and uh from the naval academy
and so I went to the NA I went into the
air force and in uh October of 19 uh uh
I just soloed when Sputnik went up the
beginning of the space age uh really got
our attention uh in the USN uh uh with
the first satellite in in in orbit. it
we u uh were
pressing on with our space program but
it was not very successful in those
early days. Uh anyway, I graduated from
flight school uh uh flight training and
was assigned to Germany 1957.
No, 19 59. And in the spring of 1959, I
went over to Germany. They picked the
first group of astronauts. NASA had been
formed. So, the space race was on, if
you will. And uh so I uh
I was just loving my job over there. And
it was a tremendous flying uh uh at
Rammstein Air Base in uh in Germany.
So, uh, the while I was on the way over,
I said I I think I mentioned the first
group of astronauts was selected, and I
didn't even have a a
an idea about being an astronaut,
but I I wanted to advance my career. And
and a year and a half later, uh, we in
Yuri Gagarin was the first human being
in space. the Russians beat us and then
Allan Shepard was the next and uh
then after that uh there was a
commitment uh to by President Kennedy to
the go to the moon on Apollo program.
Well later on I found out that uh
Wernner Brawn had written a letter to
then president uh I have a copy of that
letter and in this letter he said Mr.
president, we can't beat him here. We
can't beat him there, but we can beat
him to the moon. And so, I think based
on that letter, Kennedy accepted his
advice and committed us uh to go to the
moon. Uh and we didn't know how we're
going to do it. Uh and uh I followed the
development and there was a big debate
about whether we uh rendevued in Earth
orbit or rendevued at the moon. Well, it
was decided that uh we would take up
everything in one rocket, the lunar
module, the command module, all that you
needed to get into uh get to the moon.
So they the Saturn 5 was developed by
Marshall Space Flight Center and uh that
uh was the vehicle that every Apollo
mission that went to the moon was riding
on top of. It was 363 feet tall. It was
weighed 6 12 million pounds. It had 7 12
million pounds of thrust in the first
stage. And uh you're sitting up on top
of that thing. And uh and what you
really and windows are covered over. You
can't see outside and you're just
listening to the count. And what you
really were thinking about keep
counting.
Keep counting. I don't want to abort.
and you had a 4hour window to get off
and we had trained due to uh some
problems. We had delayed two years and
I'd been training for two years with
John Young. We were ready to go. And so
we were uh laying there and uh John was
on the left side, commander, I was on
the right side and uh and just keep
counting, keep counting. And then
t-minus 8 seconds, they lit the engines
and we started to vibrate from side to
side. No pogo, but from side to side.
And I didn't remember anybody telling me
it was supposed to shake this hard. So,
uh,
I got a little nervous and, uh, the, uh,
the, uh, and but John was very cool. It
was his second flight on the Saturn, uh,
five and he said, "We're go, Houston."
Houston said, "We're go." And so we
went. It took 8 seconds to clear the
tower. And then and then the next 2
minutes and 40 seconds or whatever it
was, uh, we accelerated out to use the
first stage. And in and in that time of
the first stage burn and we got to 35 mi
I think it was altitude and uh we'd
burned up 4 million pounds of fuel.
Over half the weight of the vehicle was
gone in the first couple of minutes and
the shaking was really and we were
accelerating four and a half times
gravity and everybody told us about we
they called it the train wreck when they
shut down the engines for you went from
four and a half gs to zero instantly. So
you stiff armed the spa the instrument
panel to get even though you were
strapped in tight. Anyway, after that,
uh, shut down start of the second stage,
boost protective cover came off and we
could see outside for the first time.
And we were going in heads down, we call
it. And I looked out the window, which
was right up here, and I could see the
deep blue of the Atlantic and fading
into the light blue of the atmosphere
into the blackness of space. And all
that was in this little window. It was
really spectacular. We orbited the earth
one and a half times over Australia. Uh
we uh reignited the uh uh third stage of
the Saturn and accelerated to escape
velocity. Uh and we were on a 26 mile uh
I mean a 26 degree uh orbit. So we got
26 north to 26,000. We fired over
Australia and we started back down to
the south. Later on, uh, we were out of
orbit and, uh, so, uh, Mattingley had
to, we had to retrieve the lunar module.
And so he did that, uh, separated. We
turned around and as I was, uh, just
floating there, uh, watching these guys
maneuver into my window on the right
side, floated the Earth. It's about
20,000 miles away. And you could see the
whole circle of the earth in the Arctic
Circle down across Canada, US, Mexico,
uh, northern part of South America. And
it was just this jewel just suspended in
the blackness of space and which we had
seen of course from Apollo pictures that
had been taken, but this view of there
it was just suspended there and you
couldn't see any evidence of
civilization. We could see the Rocky
Mountains, the southwestern United
States, the Gulf of Mexico, uh the uh
Gulf of California and that all was very
visible and but [clears throat] it
didn't you couldn't see any evidence of
people. There was no cities b visible
and uh it was uh we were on our way and
it was uh and you were just so excited
about that. I had a little feeling of I
was going to get uh air sick, but uh
that passed in about a couple hours and
I didn't didn't have to uh use the bag
uh that [clears throat] several of us
had in the past. Uh anyway, we took
three Apollo took three days to get to
the moon, 72 hours. And we got to the
moon and um uh the u
uh in went into orbit and
[clears throat] a day later we were on
the back side of the moon and we were
one hour before we were to land on the
moon. We all powered up in the lunar
module. We'd separated and Mattingly had
to change his orbit from a 60-mi
uh by 8 mile to a 60-mi circular orbit.
And he had to light the main engine. And
when he tried to do that, there was
something wrong with the control system.
And John said, "Don't burn." When John
said that, that we weren't going to land
in the next hour. And you can imagine uh
oh god I won't use the language here but
uh
that we had on board but it was uh man
you know an hour before and we're going
to abort this mission. Fortunately
mission control saved the day as they
did in Apollo. Mission control were the
heroes of Apollo. They and every flight
they overcame some problem that was
going to abort the mission and uh or
either on Apollo 13 for instance they
came up with all of the stuff that got
them back safely. So I look at mission
control as the heroes of Apollo and
their their expertise and all of the
flight controllers. So, uh,
[clears throat] we orbit, we came around
from the backside and they said, "Well,
stand by." Uh, we're looking at it and
it took two rails, two orbits, and on
the the second orbit around,
um, they said, "We we know what's wrong.
We can't fix it, but here's a
workaround, and you're go for landing on

and the flight controller made that
decision. I mean there were big NASA e

but they know make those decisions the
flight crew does I mean the flight

descent and uh I'm going to start this
uh
uh let's see here. Well, this is some

John Young. We had That's the uh in New

we had this one that worked downtown

I'm the navigator on the left side up
there. Uh [clears throat]
and this is our crew. Uh I'm on the

commander was his fourth mission, a
second on Apollo and TK Mattingley. I'm
the only one left alive uh of that crew.
Some of the Apollo crews uh all of them

the youngest and I'm 89.
And so somebody out there please
uh let's go to the moon again. So
[cheering]
I thank you. [applause]
I want to I want to pass that baton to

uh, anyway, uh, Mattingley's passed on,

Uh, we had, uh, the lunar module. We had

that's coming up uh their landing zone

if you look at the photos that have been

Uh
so I've discussed there's that direct

the evidence is overwhelming that we
landed on the moon and
[applause]
thank you. So I told uh on TV

we had a lot of experiments we put put

the uh car weighed uh 80 lbs up on the

uh and in our in my situation here uh uh

full_text: [music] Brigadier General Charles Duke, Apollo [music] 16 astronaut, US Air Force officer, and one of only 12 humans to walk on the moon, is with us today. His story is one of service, courage, and exploration at the highest level. Charlie's journey to space began as a fighter pilot, a graduate of the US Naval Academy, and later an Air Force test pilot. His precision and skill paved the way for his selection as an astronaut for NASA's Apollo program. In 1972, as a lunar module pilot for Apollo 16, Charlie became the youngest person to step foot on the moon at just 36 years old. Now, I want to pause and say, I'm 40. What have I been doing with my life? While on the lunar surface, he conducted experiments, collected samples, and contributed data that continues to shape our understanding of the moon to this day. But to me, one of the most remarkable things about Charlie is the way he connects with people. I'll never forget meeting him almost exactly eight years ago. Tomorrow, it will be exactly 8 years ago to today with my then six-year-old daughter, Millie. Like many kids, she was fascinated by space and thrilled to meet an astronaut. She asked him what he did on the moon, and when he replied, "I collected rocks." Her face fell into a pout and she declared, "That's not fair. you got to go to the moon and I didn't. I feel the exact same way. That moment captured the essence of who Charlie is. Yes, he's a legend of space exploration, but he's also someone whose warmth and humility have inspired generations to dream bigger and aim higher. Even after the Apollo 16 mission, Charlie continued to serve and advance NASA's efforts, leaving a legacy of leadership that paved the way for the future of human space flight. His contributions remind us of what can be achieved through dedication, courage, and a belief in the impossible. As we look ahead to new frontiers, whether it's returning to the moon, reaching for Mars, or safeguarding our assets in orbit, we are fortunate to stand on the shoulders of pioneers like Charlie. So, without further ado, please join me in welcoming a living legend, a man whose achievements have left an indelible mark on the history of exploration, Apollo 16 astronaut, General Charles Duke. [applause] Thank you very much. [applause] Thank you a lot. Thank you for great introduction. Thank you so much. Thank you. [cheering] Thank you very much. Thank you. Thanks so much. Please be seated. It's a great uh pleasure being here. I want to thank the space US Space Force for uh sponsoring uh uh this event and our trip. And so, uh, getting to meet, uh, those who are now recruiting for the, uh, you, uh, Air Force for the Space Force. It's, uh, great meeting you. Uh, they worked at the recruiting service at Randolph and where I worked my last, uh, eight years in the Air Force Reserve. So, it's, uh, good to I was recruiting 18 year olds back in those days. Uh it was uh in the early uh uh let's see or uh '7s I guess before that. My career is just going uh in my mind now trying to get the years straight. But uh anyway uh it's good to be here. I I want to talk about Apollo uh and how it got started uh and a little bit about uh my career. Uh I tell all a lot of young people I said just keep your antennas up because you never know where your career is going to take you. Uh and uh sensitive to urges uh and uh at the Naval Academy I had this urge to I just loved airplanes and they gave me some rides in airplanes. So uh uh I decided to back then you could go in the air force and uh from the naval academy and so I went to the NA I went into the air force and in uh October of 19 uh uh 57 I just soloed when Sputnik went up the beginning of the space age uh really got our attention uh in the USN uh uh with the first satellite in in in orbit. it we u uh were pressing on with our space program but it was not very successful in those early days. Uh anyway, I graduated from flight school uh uh flight training and was assigned to Germany 1957. No, 19 59. And in the spring of 1959, I went over to Germany. They picked the first group of astronauts. NASA had been formed. So, the space race was on, if you will. And uh so I uh I was just loving my job over there. And it was a tremendous flying uh uh at Rammstein Air Base in uh in Germany. So, uh, the while I was on the way over, I said I I think I mentioned the first group of astronauts was selected, and I didn't even have a a an idea about being an astronaut, but I I wanted to advance my career. And and a year and a half later, uh, we in 1961, Yuri Gagarin was the first human being in space. the Russians beat us and then Allan Shepard was the next and uh then after that uh there was a commitment uh to by President Kennedy to the go to the moon on Apollo program. Well later on I found out that uh Wernner Brawn had written a letter to then president uh I have a copy of that letter and in this letter he said Mr. president, we can't beat him here. We can't beat him there, but we can beat him to the moon. And so, I think based on that letter, Kennedy accepted his advice and committed us uh to go to the moon. Uh and we didn't know how we're going to do it. Uh and uh I followed the development and there was a big debate about whether we uh rendevued in Earth orbit or rendevued at the moon. Well, it was decided that uh we would take up everything in one rocket, the lunar module, the command module, all that you needed to get into uh get to the moon. So they the Saturn 5 was developed by Marshall Space Flight Center and uh that uh was the vehicle that every Apollo mission that went to the moon was riding on top of. It was 363 feet tall. It was weighed 6 12 million pounds. It had 7 12 million pounds of thrust in the first stage. And uh you're sitting up on top of that thing. And uh and what you really and windows are covered over. You can't see outside and you're just listening to the count. And what you really were thinking about keep counting. Keep counting. I don't want to abort. and you had a 4hour window to get off and we had trained due to uh some problems. We had delayed two years and I'd been training for two years with John Young.
